title: Hot vs Cold Streams & Threading
description: Cold streams, hot streams, cold-to-hot conversion, threading models across RxJava/Flow/LiveData, backpressure, and Channel vs SharedFlow

references.title: Roman Elizarov — Cold flows, hot channels

lesson:
Cold Streams — The DVD Analogy

A cold stream is like a DVD: every subscriber starts the movie from the beginning. The producer doesn't start until someone subscribes, and each subscriber gets its own independent execution.

Cold stream characteristics:
1. Producer starts on subscription — no work until someone collects/subscribes
2. Each subscriber gets an independent execution (own copy of the data)
3. Unicast — one producer per subscriber
4. Lifecycle tied to the subscriber
// Kotlin Flow — cold by default val userFlow: Flow<User> = flow { println("Fetching...") // runs EACH time someone collects val user = api.getUser() emit(user) } // Nothing happens yet — flow is just a blueprint val flow = userFlow // NOW it runs — "Fetching..." prints userFlow.collect { user -> showUser(user) } // Collecting AGAIN runs the whole thing from scratch userFlow.collect { user -> showUser(user) } // "Fetching..." prints again

Other cold streams: Observable.create { } in RxJava (the lambda runs per subscriber), Kotlin Sequence (lazy, computed on iteration), and Java Stream.

// RxJava — Observable.create is cold val observable = Observable.create<Int> { emitter -> println("Starting work") // runs per subscriber emitter.onNext(1) emitter.onNext(2) emitter.onComplete() } observable.subscribe { println(it) } // "Starting work", 1, 2 observable.subscribe { println(it) } // "Starting work", 1, 2 (fresh execution)

Hot Streams — The Live TV Analogy

A hot stream is like live TV: the broadcast is running whether you're watching or not. You tune in and see whatever is playing now — you miss what already aired.

Hot stream characteristics:
1. Producer runs independently of subscribers
2. All subscribers share the same stream of data
3. Multicast — one producer, many subscribers
4. Subscribers may miss values emitted before they subscribed

StateFlow — Always has a current value

// StateFlow: hot, always has a value, conflates (keeps latest only) class UserViewModel : ViewModel() { private val _uiState = MutableStateFlow(UiState.Loading) val uiState: StateFlow<UiState> = _uiState.asStateFlow() fun loadUser() { viewModelScope.launch { _uiState.value = UiState.Loading val user = repository.getUser() _uiState.value = UiState.Success(user) } } } // New collectors immediately get the CURRENT value // If Loading→Success emitted fast, collector only sees Success (conflation)

SharedFlow — No initial value, configurable replay

// SharedFlow: hot, no initial value, configurable buffer/replay class EventBus { private val _events = MutableSharedFlow<Event>( replay = 0, // new subscribers don't get old events extraBufferCapacity = 1, onBufferOverflow = BufferOverflow.DROP_OLDEST ) val events: SharedFlow<Event> = _events.asSharedFlow() suspend fun emit(event: Event) { _events.emit(event) } } // StateFlow IS a SharedFlow with replay=1 + distinctUntilChanged + initial value

RxJava Hot Types

// BehaviorSubject — like StateFlow (replays last value) val subject = BehaviorSubject.createDefault("initial") subject.onNext("A") subject.onNext("B") subject.subscribe { println(it) } // prints "B" (latest value) // PublishSubject — like SharedFlow(replay=0) val publish = PublishSubject.create<String>() publish.onNext("missed") // no one listening publish.subscribe { println(it) } // nothing printed for "missed" publish.onNext("received") // prints "received" // ReplaySubject — like SharedFlow(replay=N) val replay = ReplaySubject.createWithSize<String>(2) replay.onNext("A") replay.onNext("B") replay.onNext("C") replay.subscribe { println(it) } // prints "B", "C" (last 2)

LiveData — The Android-specific hot holder

// LiveData: hot, lifecycle-aware, always main thread observation class MyViewModel : ViewModel() { private val _user = MutableLiveData<User>() val user: LiveData<User> = _user fun load() { viewModelScope.launch { val result = repository.getUser() _user.value = result // must be on Main thread // _user.postValue(result) — from background thread } } } // Observers auto-removed when lifecycle reaches DESTROYED // BUT: no backpressure, no operators, not Flow-compatible

Converting Cold to Hot

You often need to take a cold data source (DB query, API call) and share the result as a hot stream for the UI. Here are the conversion patterns:

Cold Flow ──── stateIn() ────→ StateFlow (replay=1, always has value) Cold Flow ──── shareIn() ───→ SharedFlow (configurable replay) RxJava: Observable ─── share() ────→ ConnectableObservable (refcounted) Observable ─── replay(1) ──→ replays last to new subscribers Observable ─── BehaviorSubject.create() → explicit subject Key difference: stateIn/shareIn: SCOPE-aware, auto-cancel when scope dies RxJava share: manual disposal needed
// stateIn — cold Flow → StateFlow (most common in ViewModels) class SearchViewModel(repo: SearchRepository) : ViewModel() { val results: StateFlow<List<Result>> = repo.observeResults() // cold Flow .stateIn( scope = viewModelScope, // ties lifetime to ViewModel started = SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5000), // stop after 5s no collectors initialValue = emptyList() // StateFlow needs an initial value ) } // shareIn — cold Flow → SharedFlow (events, not state) val notifications: SharedFlow<Notification> = repo.notificationStream() .shareIn( scope = viewModelScope, started = SharingStarted.Lazily, // start on first collector replay = 0 // don't replay old notifications )
SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5000) is the recommended default for UI state. The 5-second timeout survives configuration changes (screen rotation) without restarting the upstream flow, but still cleans up when the user actually leaves.

Threading Model Comparison

This is one of the most frequently confused topics. Each library handles threading differently:

RxJava: subscribeOn vs observeOn

subscribeOn(io) — sets the thread where the SOURCE runs observeOn(main) — switches the thread for DOWNSTREAM operators Observable.create { ... } ← runs on IO (subscribeOn) .subscribeOn(Schedulers.io()) ← ONLY THE FIRST subscribeOn MATTERS .map { transform(it) } ← still on IO .subscribeOn(Schedulers.computation()) ← IGNORED! First one wins .observeOn(AndroidSchedulers.mainThread()) ← switches to Main .filter { it.isValid } ← runs on Main .observeOn(Schedulers.io()) ← switches BACK to IO .flatMap { api.fetch(it) } ← runs on IO .observeOn(AndroidSchedulers.mainThread()) .subscribe { updateUI(it) } ← runs on Main Rule: subscribeOn = where source runs (first wins) observeOn = where everything AFTER runs (each one switches)
// RxJava common mistake: multiple subscribeOn Observable.fromCallable { heavyComputation() } // runs on IO .subscribeOn(Schedulers.io()) // ✓ first — this is used .subscribeOn(Schedulers.computation()) // ✗ ignored! only first matters .observeOn(AndroidSchedulers.mainThread()) .subscribe { result -> showResult(result) }

Kotlin Flow: flowOn (upstream only)

flow { emit(fetchData()) } ← runs on IO (flowOn below changes this) .map { transform(it) } ← runs on IO (upstream of flowOn) .flowOn(Dispatchers.IO) ← affects everything ABOVE .filter { it.isValid } ← runs on collector's context (Main) .collect { updateUI(it) } ← runs on whatever dispatcher you collect in Rule: flowOn = changes context for everything UPSTREAM collection context = determines downstream thread (opposite direction from RxJava's observeOn!)
// Flow threading — flowOn affects UPSTREAM val results = repository.searchFlow(query) // emits on IO .map { parseResults(it) } // runs on IO (above flowOn) .flowOn(Dispatchers.IO) // ↑ everything above on IO .map { filterForUI(it) } // runs on Main (collector context) // Collect in viewModelScope → Main dispatcher viewModelScope.launch { results.collect { data -> _uiState.value = data } // Main }

LiveData: Always Main Thread

// LiveData observation is ALWAYS on main thread // No threading control at observation site viewModel.user.observe(viewLifecycleOwner) { user -> // GUARANTEED main thread — no exceptions nameTextView.text = user.name } // To do background work, use liveData builder: val user: LiveData<User> = liveData(Dispatchers.IO) { val data = repository.getUser() // runs on IO emit(data) // switches to Main automatically }

Side-by-Side Threading Comparison

AspectRxJavaKotlin FlowLiveData
Set upstream threadsubscribeOn()flowOn()liveData(Dispatchers.IO)
Switch downstreamobserveOn()Collection contextN/A (always Main)
DirectionsubscribeOn: up, observeOn: downflowOn: up onlyN/A
Multiple switchesMultiple observeOn OKMultiple flowOn OK (each scopes above it)Not possible
GotchaOnly FIRST subscribeOn mattersCannot emit from different dispatcher than flowOn without channelFlowMust postValue from BG thread

Backpressure — When the Producer Is Faster Than the Consumer

Backpressure occurs when the emitter produces items faster than the collector can process them. Each framework handles this differently:

Producer: 🏭💨💨💨 (fast) Consumer: 🐌 (slow) What happens to the items the consumer can't keep up with? ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ RxJava Observable │ NO backpressure → MissingBackpressure │ │ │ Exception if buffer overflows │ │ │ │ RxJava Flowable │ Explicit strategies: │ │ BUFFER │ Queue all items (memory risk) │ │ DROP │ Drop items that can't be processed │ │ LATEST │ Keep only most recent item │ │ ERROR │ Throw if buffer full │ │ MISSING │ No strategy — handle manually │ │ │ │ Kotlin Flow │ Suspends producer (built-in!) │ │ .buffer() │ Add buffer, producer keeps running │ │ .conflate() │ Skip intermediate values (keep latest) │ │ .collectLatest {} │ Cancel previous processing on new item │ │ │ │ StateFlow │ Built-in conflation (always latest) │ │ │ Never buffers, never suspends │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
// Flow backpressure — producer suspends by default val fastProducer = flow { repeat(1000) { i -> emit(i) // suspends if collector is busy! } } // buffer() — decouple producer and consumer with a buffer fastProducer .buffer(capacity = 64) .collect { item -> delay(100) // slow consumer — producer runs ahead up to 64 items process(item) } // conflate() — skip intermediate values, always process latest sensorReadings .conflate() .collect { reading -> delay(500) // slow UI update — skips readings emitted during processing updateGauge(reading) } // collectLatest — cancel previous processing when new value arrives searchQuery .collectLatest { query -> val results = api.search(query) // cancelled if new query arrives showResults(results) }
StateFlow is always conflated. If you emit A → B → C rapidly, a slow collector may only see C. This is perfect for UI state (you only care about the latest) but wrong for events (you'd lose navigation commands or snackbar triggers). Use SharedFlow or Channel for events.

Channel vs SharedFlow — When to Use Which

Both are hot, but they serve fundamentally different purposes:

Channel SharedFlow ─────── ────────── Delivery: Each item to ONE receiver Each item to ALL collectors (fan-out / work distribution) (multicast / event bus) Analogy: Work queue at a factory Radio broadcast (one worker picks up each task) (all radios hear the same) Buffering: Built-in rendezvous/buffered Configurable replay + buffer Back-pressure: Suspends sender when full Configurable overflow policy Completion: Can be closed Never completes Use for: • Work distribution • UI events (one-shot) • One-time delivery • Shared state changes • Producer-consumer pattern • Multiple observers • Communication between • Event bus coroutines
// Channel — each item consumed by exactly ONE receiver val workChannel = Channel<Task>(capacity = Channel.BUFFERED) // Producer scope.launch { repeat(100) { i -> workChannel.send(Task(i)) } workChannel.close() } // Multiple consumers — each task goes to ONE consumer (fan-out) repeat(4) { workerId -> scope.launch { for (task in workChannel) { println("Worker $workerId processing ${task.id}") } } } // SharedFlow — each item delivered to ALL collectors val eventBus = MutableSharedFlow<UiEvent>() // All collectors see the same event scope.launch { eventBus.collect { handleInFragmentA(it) } } scope.launch { eventBus.collect { handleInFragmentB(it) } } scope.launch { eventBus.collect { logAnalytics(it) } } eventBus.emit(UiEvent.ShowSnackbar("Saved!")) // all three see this
Rule of thumb:
• Need each event processed by exactly one handler? → Channel
• Need all observers to see every event? → SharedFlow
• Need the latest state value always available? → StateFlow
• Need lifecycle-aware observation without Flow? → LiveData (legacy)

questions.title: Cold vs Hot Identification
questions.description: Which of the following is a cold stream?
options: StateFlow — it always has a current value
SharedFlow with replay = 1
flow { } builder — the lambda runs fresh for each collector
BehaviorSubject — it replays the last value to new subscribers
questions.explanation: The flow { } builder creates a cold stream. The lambda inside runs independently for each collector, starting from scratch every time. StateFlow, SharedFlow, and BehaviorSubject are all hot — they exist independently of subscribers and share emissions across all observers.
hints: Cold = producer runs per subscriber, like pressing play on a DVD
Hot = producer runs regardless of subscribers, like live TV

questions.title: RxJava subscribeOn Gotcha
questions.description: On which threads does each stage of this RxJava chain execute?
questions.code:
Observable.fromCallable { 
    println("Source: ${Thread.currentThread().name}")
    fetchDataFromDisk()
}
    .subscribeOn(Schedulers.io())
    .map { data ->
        println("Map: ${Thread.currentThread().name}")
        transform(data)
    }
    .subscribeOn(Schedulers.computation())  // second subscribeOn
    .observeOn(AndroidSchedulers.mainThread())
    .subscribe { result ->
        println("Subscribe: ${Thread.currentThread().name}")
        showResult(result)
    }
options: Source: io, Map: computation, Subscribe: main
Source: io, Map: io, Subscribe: main — the second subscribeOn is ignored
Source: computation, Map: computation, Subscribe: main
Source: io, Map: main, Subscribe: main
questions.explanation: Only the first subscribeOn takes effect — it determines where the source and all upstream operators run. The second subscribeOn(computation) is completely ignored. observeOn(mainThread) switches everything downstream to the main thread. So: source and map both run on IO, subscribe runs on Main.
hints: subscribeOn sets where the subscription (source) happens — only the first one matters
observeOn switches the thread for everything that comes AFTER it

questions.title: Flow flowOn Direction
questions.description: What thread does each operation run on?
options: All on Default because flowOn is in the middle
Emit: Default, Map: Default, Filter: Main, Collect: Main
Emit: Main, Map: Default, Filter: Default, Collect: Main
Emit: Default, Map: Main, Filter: Main, Collect: Main
questions.explanation: flowOn changes the context for everything upstream (above it). The flow { } emission and map are upstream of flowOn(Default), so they run on Default. The filter and collect are downstream — they run on the collector's context (Main, since collected in viewModelScope). This is the opposite direction from RxJava's observeOn.
hints: flowOn affects everything ABOVE it, not below
The collector's coroutine context determines the downstream thread

questions.title: StateFlow Conflation Trap
questions.description: A ViewModel emits navigation events via StateFlow. The user taps a button twice quickly, navigating to ScreenA then ScreenB. What does the UI observer see?
options: Observer sees ScreenA then ScreenB — both events delivered
Observer sees only ScreenB — StateFlow conflates, dropping ScreenA
Observer sees ScreenA only — first value wins
App crashes due to rapid emissions
questions.explanation: StateFlow is conflated — it only keeps the latest value. Since both assignments happen synchronously before the collector resumes, the collector only sees ScreenB. The ScreenA event is lost. This is why StateFlow is wrong for one-shot events like navigation. Use SharedFlow or Channel for events instead.
hints: StateFlow keeps only the most recent value
Think about what happens if two values are set before the collector runs

questions.title: Channel vs SharedFlow Delivery
questions.description: You have two collectors listening. A Channel sends the value 42. Which collectors receive it?
options: Both A and B print 42
Either A or B prints 42, but not both — Channel is fan-out (one receiver per item)
Neither prints — Channel requires explicit receive()
App crashes because Channel doesn't support multiple consumers
questions.explanation: A Channel delivers each item to exactly one receiver (fan-out pattern). When you have multiple consumers, the item goes to whichever receiver is ready first — it's consumed and gone. If you need all observers to see every emission, use SharedFlow instead. Channel is for work distribution, SharedFlow is for event broadcasting.
hints: Think of a Channel like a work queue — one worker picks up each task
Contrast with SharedFlow which is like a radio broadcast to all listeners

questions.title: Backpressure: buffer vs conflate vs collectLatest
questions.description: A sensor emits readings every 10ms, but your processing takes 100ms. You want to always show the most recent reading and skip intermediate values during processing. Which operator is correct?
options: buffer(64) — queues readings and processes them all eventually
conflate() — skips intermediate values, always processes the latest available
collectLatest { } — cancels in-progress processing when a new value arrives
Both B and C achieve the goal, but conflate() keeps processing running while collectLatest cancels it
questions.explanation: Both conflate() and collectLatest show the latest value, but they differ: conflate() lets the current processing finish then skips to the latest — good when processing has side effects you don't want to cancel. collectLatest cancels the in-progress processing and restarts with the new value — good for search-as-you-type where stale results should be abandoned. buffer() would queue all readings, which is not what you want for a real-time display.
hints: Think about what happens to the currently-processing item when a new one arrives
conflate lets the current processing finish; collectLatest cancels it

questions.title: Cold to Hot Conversion
questions.description: What does stateIn do to this cold flow, and what value does a new collector see if it starts collecting 10 seconds later?
options: The collector gets all prices from the last 10 seconds (replay buffer)
The collector gets 0.0 (initial value) since WhileSubscribed stopped after 5s with no collectors
The collector gets the very latest price — stateIn kept running on viewModelScope
The flow restarts from the beginning when the new collector arrives
questions.explanation: SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5000) means the upstream flow stops 5 seconds after the last collector disappears. If no one collected for 10 seconds, the upstream stopped at the 5-second mark and the StateFlow reset to its initialValue of 0.0. When the Fragment starts collecting, the upstream restarts and new prices begin flowing. This is the recommended strategy — it survives configuration changes (< 5s) but cleans up when truly unused.
hints: WhileSubscribed(5000) stops the upstream 5 seconds after the last collector goes away
What value does a StateFlow have after the upstream stops?

questions.title: Hot vs Cold Mental Model
questions.description: Explain the DVD vs Live TV analogy for cold vs hot streams, and name two cold and two hot stream types from Kotlin/RxJava.
questions.answer: A cold stream is like a DVD — each subscriber starts from the beginning and gets their own independent copy of the data. The producer doesn't run until someone subscribes. Examples: Kotlin flow { } builder, RxJava Observable.create { }. A hot stream is like live TV — it broadcasts regardless of subscribers, and you tune in to whatever is currently playing. All subscribers share the same stream. Examples: StateFlow, SharedFlow, BehaviorSubject, LiveData.
questions.explanation: The key insight is about sharing: cold streams create a new producer per subscriber (unicast), while hot streams share one producer across all subscribers (multicast). This matters for resource usage — a cold network call flow collected by 3 fragments makes 3 network calls. Converting to hot with shareIn makes it one call shared across all.
hints: Think about what happens when a SECOND subscriber joins
Does the producer restart or continue from where it is?
